question_text: Food preservation aims to prevent or slow down spoilage and extend the shelf life of perishable foods by controlling specific factors responsible for deterioration — which of the following is NOT a recognised principle of food preservation used in commercial food processing?

options.text: Asepsis — keeping foods free from microbial contamination during processing and packaging
Deliberate addition of toxic heavy metals like lead to extend shelf life indefinitely
Anaerobic conditions — removing oxygen to prevent growth of aerobic spoilage microbes
Temperature control — using cold to slow microbial activity or heat to destroy microbes

explanation: Food preservation is based on scientifically established principles: (1) asepsis (keeping microbes out); (2) removal of microbes (filtration, washing); (3) anaerobic conditions (vacuum/modified atmosphere packaging); (4) temperature control (refrigeration, freezing, heat processing); (5) moisture reduction (drying, concentration); (6) chemical preservatives at safe levels; (7) pH adjustment (acidification); (8) irradiation. Adding toxic heavy metals like lead is absolutely prohibited under food safety laws (FSSAI in India, FDA globally) — it causes severe health hazards and is not a preservation principle. Options A, C, and D are all valid principles universally applied in food industry.

elimination_hints.hint: Adding toxic substances contradicts the entire purpose of food preservation (safe food).
All other options describe scientifically accepted preservation principles.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Canning is one of the oldest and most widely used food preservation techniques that involves hermetic sealing and heat processing — which French confectioner is credited as the 'Father of Canning' for developing the preservation principle in 1809?

options.text: Louis Pasteur (developed pasteurisation of milk)
Nicolas Appert (invented canning by heating food in sealed glass jars in 1809)
Clarence Birdseye (developed quick freezing technology in 1920s)
Peter Durand (patented the tin can in 1810 based on Appert's principle)

explanation: Nicolas Appert, a French confectioner, is credited as the 'Father of Canning'. In 1809 he won a 12,000-franc prize from Napoleon Bonaparte's government for developing a method to preserve food for military campaigns — he discovered that heating food in sealed glass jars prevented spoilage (even though the microbial basis wasn't understood until Pasteur later). Louis Pasteur developed pasteurisation (1864) of milk. Clarence Birdseye pioneered modern quick freezing (1920s). Peter Durand adapted Appert's method using tin-coated iron cans (1810) — complementary innovations. Appert's book 'The Art of Preserving All Kinds of Animal and Vegetable Substances for Several Years' (1810) laid the foundation for the modern canning industry.

elimination_hints.hint: Louis Pasteur developed pasteurisation (different from canning), 55 years after Appert.
Birdseye pioneered freezing (20th century technology).

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Pasteurisation is a mild heat treatment designed to destroy pathogenic microbes in liquid foods while preserving flavour and nutrition — which of the following correctly matches HTST pasteurisation parameters commonly used for milk in commercial dairies?

options.text: 63°C for 30 minutes — the LTLT (Low Temperature Long Time) method
72°C for 15 seconds followed by rapid cooling to below 4°C — HTST method
135°C for 2 seconds — UHT (Ultra High Temperature) method
100°C for 10 minutes — the boiling method

explanation: HTST (High Temperature Short Time) pasteurisation heats milk at 72°C for 15 seconds in plate heat exchangers, followed by rapid cooling to below 4°C. This kills pathogens like Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Brucella, Coxiella burnetii, and Listeria while retaining milk flavour and most vitamins. LTLT (Low Temperature Long Time) uses 63°C for 30 minutes — older batch method. UHT (Ultra-High Temperature) uses 135°C for 2 seconds enabling ambient-shelf-stable milk (tetra pak). Boiling at 100°C is not true pasteurisation. Commercial dairies in India (Amul, Mother Dairy, Nestlé) predominantly use HTST. Alkaline phosphatase test indicates adequate pasteurisation.

elimination_hints.hint: 63°C/30 min is LTLT, not HTST — different method name.
UHT (135°C/2 sec) is a distinct technology producing shelf-stable milk.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: The '12D concept' in commercial canning of low-acid foods (pH >4.5) targets a specific pathogen spore whose destruction is the benchmark for sterilisation adequacy — which microorganism serves as this reference target in thermal processing calculations?

options.text: Escherichia coli as an indicator of faecal contamination
Clostridium botulinum spores producing the deadly botulinum toxin
Lactobacillus acidophilus, a beneficial lactic acid bacterium
Saccharomyces cerevisiae, the bread-making yeast

explanation: The 12D concept targets Clostridium botulinum spores as the reference pathogen in thermal processing of low-acid canned foods (pH >4.5). 12D means reducing a hypothetical initial population of 10¹² spores to 10⁰ (one spore) — a 12 log-cycle reduction, effectively eliminating food poisoning risk. This is because C. botulinum produces botulinum toxin — the most potent biological toxin known — causing botulism with <1 µg fatal doses. In low-acid foods (most vegetables, meat, fish), C. botulinum can grow anaerobically in sealed cans if not properly sterilised. The D-value (decimal reduction time) at 121°C for C. botulinum is approximately 0.21 minutes, giving an F₀ minimum of 2.52 minutes for 12D. In acid foods (pH<4.5 like fruit juices, tomato), C. botulinum cannot grow, so lower thermal processing suffices.

elimination_hints.hint: E. coli is a non-spore-forming bacterium easily destroyed at lower temperatures.
Yeast and Lactobacillus are not pathogens targeted in low-acid canning sterilisation.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Foods are classified into acid and low-acid categories based on their natural pH, which fundamentally determines the sterilisation intensity required during canning — which pH value is the universally accepted dividing line between acid and low-acid foods as per international food processing standards?

options.text: pH 7.0 (neutral pH serving as natural classification boundary)
pH 4.5 (the critical value below which Clostridium botulinum cannot grow)
pH 5.5 (average pH of most fresh vegetables and meats)
pH 6.0 (the pH of distilled water after exposure to atmospheric CO₂)

explanation: pH 4.5 is the internationally recognised dividing line between acid foods (pH ≤4.5) and low-acid foods (pH >4.5) based on the growth characteristics of Clostridium botulinum. This pathogen cannot grow or produce toxin below pH 4.5 — so acid foods need only mild pasteurisation (85–100°C) while low-acid foods require commercial sterilisation in retorts at 115–125°C under pressure (to target C. botulinum spores). Acid foods: most fruits, juices, tomato (pH 4.0–4.3), pickles. Low-acid foods: most vegetables (peas, corn, beans), meat, fish, milk, soup. Tomato is a border case sometimes requiring acidification. This dichotomy is the single most important classification in canning science and is codified in FSSAI regulations and FDA 21 CFR Part 113/114.

elimination_hints.hint: pH 7.0 is neutral but has no biological meaning in food safety.
C. botulinum's pH growth limit (4.5) is what drives the classification.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Drying is a widely used preservation method that reduces water content to a level below which microbial growth cannot occur — what is the typical moisture content target for dried fruits and vegetables to ensure stable storage without spoilage?

options.text: 20–25 percent moisture content for stable storage over months
5–10 percent moisture content with corresponding water activity (Aw) below 0.6
50–60 percent moisture content allowing semi-moist texture
0 percent moisture (completely anhydrous state) required for stability

explanation: Dried fruits and vegetables are typically brought to 5–10% moisture content (sometimes up to 15% for some products like raisins) to ensure microbial stability. At this moisture level, the water activity (Aw) drops below 0.60 — below which no microorganism can grow (moulds require Aw >0.60, yeasts >0.75, bacteria >0.85). Dehydrated products at Aw 0.30–0.50 have the longest shelf life. 20–25% moisture (A) would allow mould growth; 50–60% (C) is wet and unstable; 0% moisture (D) is impossible to achieve practically and damages product quality through excessive heat/oxidation. Water activity (Aw) is a more accurate predictor of spoilage than moisture content itself.

elimination_hints.hint: Higher moisture (25%, 60%) supports microbial growth — not stable.
Absolute dryness (0%) is not practical and damages product quality.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Individual Quick Freezing (IQF) is a modern technology that rapidly freezes food items individually to preserve quality better than conventional slow freezing — which specific quality advantage does IQF provide over slow freezing of fruits and vegetables?

options.text: Formation of large ice crystals that improve the crunchy texture after thawing
Formation of small ice crystals that minimise cell wall damage, preserving texture and reducing drip loss on thawing
Complete destruction of all enzymes without any blanching requirement
Removal of all nutritional components preventing any oxidation during storage

explanation: IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) exposes foods to very low temperatures (-30 to -40°C) rapidly, forming small intracellular ice crystals that cause minimal damage to cell walls and membranes. On thawing, these products retain their original texture, colour, and flavour with minimal drip loss. Slow freezing at higher temperatures (-15 to -20°C) forms large ice crystals that puncture cell walls, causing mushy texture and excessive drip loss after thawing. IQF is used for green peas, berries, cubed carrots, paneer, seafood, and ready-to-cook vegetables. Foods still require blanching before freezing (to inactivate enzymes like peroxidase that cause quality deterioration during frozen storage). IQF products command premium prices due to superior quality.

elimination_hints.hint: Large ice crystals damage cell structure — opposite of quality preservation.
Blanching is still required before IQF; freezing alone doesn't inactivate enzymes.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Jam is a preserved fruit product made by boiling fruit pulp with sugar, and it must meet specific FSSAI compositional standards to be labelled as 'jam' in the Indian market — what is the minimum total soluble solids (TSS) content required for a product to qualify as jam?

options.text: 45 percent TSS (for low-sugar variants marketed as diet foods)
55 percent TSS (the general target for fruit preserves)
68.5 percent TSS (FSSAI minimum for classification as jam in India)
85 percent TSS (required only for concentrates like honey)

explanation: As per FSSAI Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations 2011, jam must contain a minimum of 68.5% TSS (measured by refractometer at 20°C) and not less than 45% fruit content. The high sugar concentration (>68.5%) creates a high osmotic pressure that inhibits microbial growth, serving as the primary preservation mechanism. Jam setting depends on sugar-acid-pectin balance — 68.5% sugar + 1.0–1.5% pectin + acidified to pH 2.8–3.5 at 104–105°C endpoint gives firm gelation. Below 68.5% TSS, the product would require refrigeration or chemical preservatives. Low-sugar 'diet' jams (40–50% TSS) need added preservatives like potassium sorbate.

elimination_hints.hint: 45% and 55% TSS are below the FSSAI legal jam standard — would not qualify.
85% TSS describes honey, not jam — different product standard.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Jelly making requires a delicate balance of three critical factors to achieve proper gel formation, and pectin is a key component extracted from fruit cell walls — which category of pectin is best suited for high-sugar jellies and sets rapidly at high TSS and low acid?

options.text: High Methoxyl (HM) Rapid Set pectin — degree of esterification >70%, sets at 68–75% TSS and pH 2.8–3.3
Low Methoxyl (LM) pectin — degree of esterification <50%, sets with calcium ions regardless of sugar level
Pectinesterase enzyme — actually degrades pectin preventing gel formation
Amidated pectin — used only for microbial fermentation applications

explanation: High Methoxyl (HM) Rapid Set pectin (degree of esterification DE >70%, typically 70–76%) is used for traditional jams and jellies. It forms gels in high-sugar (>65% TSS) and low-pH (2.8–3.3) environments through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. 'Rapid Set' grades gel at higher temperature (88–90°C), suitable for jams with fruit pieces where premature sinking needs prevention. 'Slow Set' HM pectin (DE 50–70%) gels at lower temperature (70–80°C) for clear jellies. Low Methoxyl (LM) pectin (DE <50%) gels through calcium bridges rather than sugar-acid — used for low-sugar 'diet' jams. Pectinesterase is an enzyme that de-methylates pectin (useful in low-sugar products but would fail for traditional high-sugar jelly).

elimination_hints.hint: LM pectin targets low-sugar diet products, not traditional high-sugar jellies.
Pectinesterase degrades pectin — opposite of what's needed for gel formation.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Food preservation in India is regulated by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) which sets compositional, labelling, and safety standards for processed foods — under which Act was FSSAI established?

options.text: The Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, 1954 (superseded)
The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (with FSSAI becoming operational in 2011)
The Consumer Protection Act, 2019
The Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937

explanation: FSSAI was established under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, which superseded and consolidated eight earlier food laws including the Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (1954), Fruit Products Order (1955), Meat Food Products Order (1973), Milk and Milk Products Order (1992), and others. FSSAI became operational in August 2011. It is headquartered in New Delhi and operates under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. FSSAI sets food safety standards, issues licences (FSSAI number mandatory on packaging), conducts risk assessment, and implements food labelling regulations. It works closely with state food safety commissioners for enforcement. The Consumer Protection Act is a separate consumer rights framework; AGMARK operates under AP(G&M) Act for agricultural products' quality grading.

elimination_hints.hint: PFA 1954 was superseded — FSSAI operates under the newer 2006 Act.
AGMARK is quality grading for agricultural produce, not a food safety regulator.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each chemical preservative with its correct FSSAI class and primary food application in commercial preserved food products.
column_a: Sugar at high concentration (68.5% and above)
column_b: Class I preservative — natural agent used at 68.5% TSS in jams and jellies for osmotic preservation
Class II chemical preservative — inhibits yeasts and moulds in low-sugar bakery, cheese, and wine products at 200–1000 ppm
Class II chemical preservative — used at maximum 750 ppm in fruit juices, squashes, and pickles to inhibit bacteria and moulds
Class II chemical preservative — used at maximum 350 ppm as SO₂ in dried fruits, wine, and fruit juices against microbes and enzymatic browning

correct_mapping.Sodium benzoate / Benzoic acid: Class II chemical preservative — used at maximum 750 ppm in fruit juices, squashes, and pickles to inhibit bacteria and moulds
correct_mapping.Sulphur dioxide / Sodium metabisulphite: Class II chemical preservative — used at maximum 350 ppm as SO₂ in dried fruits, wine, and fruit juices against microbes and enzymatic browning
correct_mapping.Sugar at high concentration (68.5% and a: Class I preservative — natural agent used at 68.5% TSS in jams and jellies for osmotic preservation
correct_mapping.Sorbic acid / Potassium sorbate: Class II chemical preservative — inhibits yeasts and moulds in low-sugar bakery, cheese, and wine products at 200–1000 ppm

options.text: Sodium benzoate→Class II 750 ppm juices; SO₂→Class II 350 ppm dried fruits; Sugar→Class I 68.5% jams; Sorbic acid→Class II bakery/cheese
Sodium benzoate→Class I jams; SO₂→Class II juices; Sugar→Class II bakery; Sorbic acid→Class II dried fruits
Sodium benzoate→Class II dried fruits; SO₂→Class I jams; Sugar→Class II bakery; Sorbic acid→Class II juices
Sodium benzoate→Class II bakery; SO₂→Class II 350 ppm juices; Sugar→Class II 68.5% jams; Sorbic acid→Class I

explanation: FSSAI classifies preservatives into: (1) Class I — natural preservatives like salt, sugar (68.5% TSS for jam), vinegar, oil — generally regarded as safe with no quantity limits; (2) Class II — chemical preservatives with maximum prescribed limits: benzoic acid/sodium benzoate (750 ppm) for juices/squashes/pickles; sulphur dioxide (350 ppm) for dried fruits, wines, cut fruits preventing enzymatic browning; sorbic acid (200–1000 ppm) for cheese, bakery products, wine against yeasts/moulds; propionic acid for bread against moulds; nitrites for cured meats. Some products can use Class II preservatives either singly or in combination (but not both benzoate and SO₂ together in squashes).

elimination_hints.hint: Sugar as Class I natural preservative is a core FSSAI classification.
Benzoate at 750 ppm for squashes is a textbook permissible limit.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each food preservation method with its primary mechanism of action that prevents microbial spoilage and extends shelf life of the processed product.
column_b: Destruction of microorganisms and spores by high temperature (115–125°C) under pressure in hermetically sealed containers
Reduction of water activity (Aw < 0.6) by removing moisture, denying microbes the water needed for growth
Suspension of microbial growth by maintaining temperature below freezing; microbes dormant but not killed
Combination of high salt (10–15%) and low pH (below 4.0 from acetic/lactic acid) creating hostile environment for microbes

correct_mapping.Canning (retort sterilisation): Destruction of microorganisms and spores by high temperature (115–125°C) under pressure in hermetically sealed containers
correct_mapping.Freezing at -18°C: Suspension of microbial growth by maintaining temperature below freezing; microbes dormant but not killed
correct_mapping.Sun drying / Mechanical drying: Reduction of water activity (Aw < 0.6) by removing moisture, denying microbes the water needed for growth
correct_mapping.Pickling in brine and vinegar: Combination of high salt (10–15%) and low pH (below 4.0 from acetic/lactic acid) creating hostile environment for microbes

options.text: Canning→Heat sterilisation; Freezing→Suspends growth; Drying→Reduces Aw; Pickling→High salt + low pH
Canning→High salt; Freezing→Reduces Aw; Drying→Suspends growth; Pickling→Heat sterilisation
Canning→Reduces Aw; Freezing→High salt; Drying→Heat sterilisation; Pickling→Suspends growth
Canning→Suspends growth; Freezing→High salt; Drying→Suspends growth; Pickling→Reduces Aw

explanation: Each preservation method works through a distinct mechanism: (1) Canning destroys microbes by heat (115–125°C in pressure retorts for low-acid foods); the sealed container prevents recontamination. (2) Freezing at -18°C suspends microbial growth (microbes enter dormancy but are not killed — food safety depends on maintaining cold chain until consumption). (3) Drying reduces water activity below 0.6 — microbes cannot grow without available water even if physically present. (4) Pickling combines multiple hurdles — high salt creates osmotic stress, acidic pH (<4.0) from acetic acid (vinegar) or lactic fermentation inhibits growth, sometimes augmented by chemical preservatives. This 'hurdle technology' is central to food preservation science (developed by Leistner).

elimination_hints.hint: Heat sterilisation is uniquely canning's mechanism — direct matching.
Freezing never 'kills' microbes — it only suspends growth; this is a classic distinction.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each preserved food product with its correct minimum compositional standard as laid down by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) for legal classification and labelling.
column_b: Minimum 25% fruit juice and 10% TSS — diluted concentration for direct consumption
Minimum 45% fruit juice and 40–50% TSS — requires dilution before consumption
Minimum 65% TSS with at least 25% fruit juice content used for reconstitution to beverage
Minimum 68.5% TSS and not less than 45% fruit content for classification as jam

correct_mapping.Jam: Minimum 68.5% TSS and not less than 45% fruit content for classification as jam
correct_mapping.Squash: Minimum 45% fruit juice and 40–50% TSS — requires dilution before consumption
correct_mapping.Ready-to-serve fruit beverage: Minimum 25% fruit juice and 10% TSS — diluted concentration for direct consumption
correct_mapping.Fruit juice concentrate: Minimum 65% TSS with at least 25% fruit juice content used for reconstitution to beverage

options.text: Jam→68.5% TSS/45% fruit; Squash→45% juice/40-50% TSS; RTS→25% juice/10% TSS; Concentrate→65% TSS/25% juice
Jam→25% juice/10% TSS; Squash→68.5% TSS/45% fruit; RTS→45% juice/40-50% TSS; Concentrate→65% TSS/25% juice
Jam→65% TSS/25% juice; Squash→25% juice/10% TSS; RTS→68.5% TSS/45% fruit; Concentrate→45% juice
Jam→45% juice; Squash→65% TSS; RTS→68.5% TSS; Concentrate→25% juice/10% TSS

explanation: FSSAI specifies minimum standards: (1) Jam — 68.5% TSS + 45% fruit content; (2) Squash — 45% fruit juice + 40–50% TSS (diluted 1:3 or 1:4 before drinking); (3) Ready-to-Serve (RTS) beverage — 25% juice + 10% TSS (consumed as is); (4) Fruit juice concentrate — 65% TSS + 25% juice (reconstituted in water). Other standards: fruit nectar (20% juice + 15% TSS); fruit drink (10% juice + 10% TSS); cordial (clarified squash similar standards). These compositional standards protect consumers from adulteration and ensure product quality consistency across brands.

elimination_hints.hint: Jam's 68.5% TSS is the iconic FSSAI number — easy to identify.
RTS beverages are drunk directly — logically low TSS (10%).

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Analyse the Assertion and Reason below about the mandatory blanching step before freezing vegetables and choose the option that best describes their relationship.
assertion: Vegetables must be blanched in hot water at 85–95°C for 1–3 minutes before freezing to ensure long-term quality during frozen storage.
reason: Blanching inactivates oxidative enzymes like peroxidase, catalase, and polyphenol oxidase that would otherwise remain active at freezing temperatures, causing off-flavours, colour deterioration, and nutrient loss during storage.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — blanching is a mandatory pre-treatment before freezing vegetables (peas, beans, spinach, carrots, broccoli, cauliflower). The reason correctly explains the biochemical basis: oxidative enzymes (peroxidase, catalase, polyphenol oxidase, lipoxygenase) remain active even at freezing temperatures, slowly degrading food quality over months of storage — producing off-flavours, discoloration (browning), and vitamin loss. Blanching at 85–95°C for 1–3 minutes denatures these enzymes permanently. Peroxidase is the most heat-resistant enzyme and serves as the indicator — if peroxidase is inactivated, other enzymes certainly are too. After blanching, immediate cooling in ice water prevents overcooking. Fruits are usually not blanched to preserve fresh flavour; sugar syrup or ascorbic acid dip is used instead.

elimination_hints.hint: Blanching before freezing is a textbook mandatory step — well-documented.
Peroxidase's role as the indicator enzyme is a standard food science fact.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate the Assertion and Reason below about the preservation mechanism of pickles and choose the option that correctly describes their relationship.
assertion: Traditional mango and lemon pickles preserved in oil and salt remain safe at ambient temperature for over a year without refrigeration despite containing highly perishable fresh ingredients.
reason: The combined action of high salt concentration (10–15%), low pH from vinegar or lactic fermentation, and oil surface layer acting as an anaerobic seal creates multiple hurdles that prevent microbial growth, following the hurdle technology principle.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — traditional Indian pickles can have shelf life of 1–2 years at ambient temperature. The reason correctly explains the mechanism using the hurdle technology concept (developed by Lothar Leistner): no single factor is strong enough to prevent spoilage alone, but the combination of multiple mild hurdles is highly effective. The hurdles in pickles are: (1) high salt (10–15%) creating osmotic stress and reducing water activity (Aw to 0.70–0.80); (2) low pH (below 4.0 from acetic acid in vinegar pickles or lactic acid from fermentation) inhibiting most spoilage microbes; (3) spices containing antimicrobial compounds (allyl isothiocyanate in mustard, curcumin in turmeric); (4) oil layer on top creating anaerobic conditions and physical barrier. Together these hurdles make pickles remarkably shelf-stable — a brilliant traditional application of modern food microbiology principles.

elimination_hints.hint: Traditional pickle's shelf stability is empirically well-established.
Hurdle technology is the standard explanation in modern food microbiology.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below about the role of Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI) in Indian food preservation and determine their relationship.
assertion: The Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI) based at Mysuru, Karnataka is India's premier research organisation for food technology and preservation science.
reason: CFTRI under the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) conducts research on food processing, develops preservation technologies, trains industry personnel, and transfers technology to commercial food manufacturers through licensing and consultancy.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — CFTRI (established 1950 at Mysuru) is India's apex food technology research institute. The reason correctly explains its role: CFTRI operates under CSIR, conducts research across cereals/pulses/fruits/vegetables/dairy/meat/beverages, develops preservation technologies, trains industry personnel, and transfers technology through licensing to hundreds of food manufacturers. CFTRI technologies have supported the Indian food industry in areas like fortified foods, space food for ISRO, bakery improvements, traditional food standardisation, and low-cost preservation methods for farmer-based processing. CFTRI also operates Food Science programmes (M.Sc, Ph.D) through its affiliated institutions. The reason directly justifies the assertion by listing CFTRI's actual functions.

elimination_hints.hint: CFTRI Mysuru's position as India's food technology research apex is widely recognised.
Research, training, and technology transfer are CFTRI's documented mandate.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct chronological sequence for commercial canning of fruits in syrup from receiving raw fruit to finished canned product in a commercial cannery.
items: Receive, wash, sort, grade, peel, core, and cut fruits to uniform size as per specification
Fill prepared fruit pieces into cans along with hot sugar syrup (40–55° Brix) leaving adequate 6–9 mm headspace
Exhaust cans through hot water bath or steam tunnel to expel air, then seal with double-seaming machine
Sterilise sealed cans in retort at 100°C (acid fruits) or 115–121°C (low-acid foods) for calculated time, cool rapidly, label and store

options.text: Receive, wash, prepare fruit → Fill cans with syrup → Exhaust and seal → Sterilise, cool, label
Fill cans with syrup → Receive, wash, prepare fruit → Sterilise, cool, label → Exhaust and seal
Exhaust and seal → Sterilise, cool, label → Receive, wash, prepare fruit → Fill cans with syrup
Sterilise, cool, label → Exhaust and seal → Fill cans with syrup → Receive, wash, prepare fruit

explanation: Commercial canning sequence: (1) Receive fresh fruits → wash → sort by quality → grade by size → peel and core mechanically → cut to uniform pieces (slices, cubes, halves). (2) Fill prepared fruit into pre-sanitised cans, add hot sugar syrup at 40–55°Brix (varies by product), leaving 6–9 mm headspace for thermal expansion and vacuum development. (3) Exhaust cans to remove air (passing through hot water or steam tunnel at 90–95°C for 5–10 minutes, raising product centre temp to 70–80°C), then immediately seal cans with double-seaming machine (creates hermetic seal). (4) Sterilise in retort at appropriate temperature-time combination (e.g., peaches in syrup at 100°C for 25 minutes, green peas at 118°C for 40 minutes), rapidly cool in cold water (below 40°C), inspect, label, and palletise for distribution. Following this sequence ensures commercial sterility and quality.

elimination_hints.hint: Raw material must be received and prepared first — nothing can precede it.
Cans must be filled before being exhausted — can't exhaust empty cans.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct chronological order for commercial preparation of mixed fruit jam from fresh fruit to packaged product meeting FSSAI standards.
items: Extract and measure fruit pulp from cleaned, sorted, and de-skinned/cored fruits in 45:55 fruit-to-sugar ratio
Cook fruit pulp with sugar, added pectin (1%), and citric acid (0.2–0.5%) till endpoint temperature 104–105°C reaches 68.5% TSS
Fill hot jam into sterilised glass jars leaving 6 mm headspace, immediately cap and invert for top sterilisation
Cool jars in water bath, label with FSSAI logo and batch details, and pack for dispatch to wholesale/retail markets

options.text: Extract fruit pulp → Cook with sugar/pectin/acid → Hot fill and cap → Cool, label, pack
Cook with sugar → Extract fruit pulp → Cool, label, pack → Hot fill and cap
Hot fill and cap → Cool, label, pack → Extract fruit pulp → Cook with sugar
Cool, label, pack → Hot fill and cap → Cook with sugar → Extract fruit pulp

explanation: Jam preparation sequence: (1) Extract fruit pulp from washed, sorted, peeled, and deseeded/cored fruits; weigh pulp and add sugar in 45:55 ratio (to achieve 68.5% TSS endpoint). (2) Cook the pulp-sugar mixture in a steam-jacketed kettle, adding 1.0% pectin and 0.2–0.5% citric acid (adjusts pH to 3.0–3.5 for gel formation); continue cooking until endpoint 104–105°C (or TSS 68.5% by refractometer). (3) Hot fill (at 85–90°C) into pre-sterilised glass jars leaving 6 mm headspace, immediately cap with lids, and invert for 5 minutes to sterilise the lid interior through hot jam contact. (4) Cool jars in water bath or air cooling zone, label with FSSAI logo, batch number, manufacturing/expiry dates, and pack in corrugated boxes for distribution. Following this sequence ensures a legally compliant, high-quality, shelf-stable product.

elimination_hints.hint: Fruit pulp must be extracted before cooking — cannot cook what doesn't exist yet.
Labelling and packing is always the last step in any food processing operation.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate whether the following statement is true or false: Water activity (Aw) is a more accurate predictor of food spoilage than total moisture content because it reflects the availability of water to microorganisms, and no microbial growth occurs below Aw of 0.60.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is true. Water activity (Aw) = vapour pressure of water in food / vapour pressure of pure water, measured on a 0–1.0 scale. It measures 'free water' available for microbial growth and chemical reactions, unlike total moisture content which includes bound water. Microbial growth thresholds: bacteria need Aw >0.85 (most pathogens >0.90), yeasts >0.75, moulds >0.60. Below Aw 0.60, no microorganism can grow — this is the universal safety threshold for shelf-stable dried foods. Two foods with similar moisture content can have very different Aw depending on binding to solutes; this is why jam (68.5% sugar, Aw ~0.80, moderate moisture but high solute) is stable while fresh fruit (80% water, Aw ~0.98) is not. Aw concept (developed by Scott in 1957) is fundamental to modern food preservation science.

elimination_hints.hint: The Aw < 0.60 threshold for microbial safety is universally cited in food science.
Scott's water activity concept is a cornerstone of modern food microbiology.

subject: Agriculture

question_text: Study the diagram showing major food preservation methods along with their primary mechanisms of action and typical food applications, then identify the correct method-mechanism-application combination as commonly practised in commercial food processing.

options.text: Canning — heat sterilisation at 115–125°C in hermetic cans — used for low-acid foods like peas, meat, and soups
Freezing at -18°C — kills all microorganisms including spores permanently — used for low-acid canned goods
Drying to 5–10% moisture — reduces temperature below freezing — used for pasteurising milk
Pickling — sterilises through UHT heat treatment — used exclusively for freezing vegetables

explanation: From the diagram, canning is correctly identified with heat sterilisation at 115–125°C in hermetically sealed cans applied to low-acid foods (peas, meat, soups, vegetables). Freezing at -18°C suspends microbial growth (does not kill — microbes become dormant but may revive on thawing); freezing is used for vegetables/meat/fish, not canned goods. Drying reduces moisture content to 5–10% and water activity to below 0.6 (it doesn't lower temperature); it's used for dried fruits, vegetables, spices — not pasteurising milk (which uses HTST/UHT). Pickling uses high salt and low pH (hurdle technology), not UHT. Only option A correctly aligns method, mechanism, and application.

elimination_hints.hint: Freezing never 'kills' microbes — it suspends their activity; rules out B.
Drying doesn't lower temperature — it removes moisture; rules out C.

subject: Agriculture
